summary: Joel Spolsky comments on three unrelated topics: the failure of Raging Search (an AltaVista spinoff) to match Google's search quality despite copying its design, Juno's invasive privacy policy that tracks extensive user data for millions of Americans, and a brief personal note about his UI book being completed. He reflects on whether he would have refused to implement Juno's tracking features on principle had he still worked there, but honestly admits his integrity was never actually tested since he had already quit.
key_points: Copying a product's design (as Raging Search did with Google) doesn't replicate the underlying quality that makes it work
Juno's privacy policy represents a troubling erosion of user privacy by combining personal identity with detailed browsing data
Spolsky questions whether he would have taken a principled stand against implementing invasive tracking, but admits he never had to face that test
Even principled refusal by employees rarely changes corporate decisions — management would simply pressure dissenters into compliance
money_quotes: This steady erosion of privacy dismays me.
The Juno database will have your name and address and the web pages you visited, for 3 million Americans who use the service every month.
I would like to think that I would have refused on principle, even if it cost me my job.
Then again, it's hard to say "I would have taken a stand," since I wasn't really there, and I didn't really take a stand.
Lucky for me, I quit way back in November, and so my integrity never stood the test.
Management would just gather us all in a room and scream at us.
key_insight: It's easy to claim you would have taken a principled stand against unethical practices when you never actually had to face the choice.
